evidence: The docstring asks for the 3-uniform ordinal Ramsey partition relation c -> (beta, n)^3_2, and the file defines OrdinalCardinalRamsey3 as 'the 3-uniform ordinal Ramsey property'.
The docstring states 'Let c (frak) be the cardinality of the continuum' and the statement colors triples of the ordinal (𝔠).ord.
The problem quantifies over countable ordinals beta ('let beta be a countable ordinal') and asks for red-monochromatic subsets of a given order type.
The statement concerns f(n) = min gcd(n, C(n,k)) over binomial coefficients; the reference is Erdos-Szekeres 'Some number theoretic problems on binomial coefficients'.
The docstring defines f(n) as the minimum of gcd(n, binom(n,k)) over 1 < k <= n/2, so the gcd is the central object of the problem.
Part (b) asks whether f(n) = min gcd(n, C(n,k)) exceeds sqrt(n) infinitely often for composite n, with C(n,k) the binomial coefficient.
Part (b)'s docstring restates f(n) = min over 1 < k <= n/2 of gcd(n, binom(n,k)).
Part (c) bounds f(n) = min gcd(n, C(n,k)) by n/(log n)^A, with the binomial coefficient at the heart of the definition.
Part (c)'s docstring restates f(n) = min over 1 < k <= n/2 of gcd(n, binom(n,k)).
The docstring asks about a finite unit distance graph G in R^2 (O'Donnell constructed unit distance graphs of chromatic number 4 and large girth).
The question is whether girth >= k forces chi(G) <= 3; the erdosproblems tag is 'chromatic number'.
The hypothesis of the statement is that G has girth >= k, with O'Donnell's disproof via graphs of arbitrarily large girth.
The problem asks whether any finite Sidon set A can be embedded in a perfect difference set; the erdosproblems tags are 'additive combinatorics, sidon sets'.
The statement concerns embedding into a perfect difference set modulo n (IsPerfectDifferenceSet), citing Hall's 'Cyclic projective planes' counterexample.
The counterexample set {1, 2, 4, 8, 13} named 'mian_chowla' in the declaration is the initial segment of the Mian-Chowla sequence, shown by Alexeev-Mixon not to extend to any perfect difference set.
The file header is 'Erdős Problem 723: The prime power conjecture' and the theorem states exactly it: every finite projective plane has prime power order.
The docstring asks 'If there is a finite projective plane of order n then must n be a prime power?'.
The conjectured conclusion is IsPrimePow pp.order, i.e. that the order of the plane is a prime power.
The docstring states the Bruck-Ryser result that a projective plane order n with n = 1 or 2 mod 4 must be a sum of two squares — the projective-plane case of the Bruck-Ryser-Chowla theorem.
The conjectured asymptotic sum over primes 1/p ~ (loglog n)/2 is a restricted-residue refinement of 'the classical estimate of Mertens' cited in the same file.
The docstring reads 'The classical estimate of Mertens states that sum_{p<=n} 1/p ~ loglog n', which is Mertens' second theorem.
The problem asks whether ((n+k)!)^2 divides (2n)! for infinitely many n; the erdosproblems tags are 'number theory, factorials'.
The statement asks for integers with a! b! dividing n! (a+b-n)! in the a+b = n + O(log n) regime; the erdosproblems tags are 'number theory, factorials'.
